case_name: Dadapeera S/O. Abdul Salam Sab vs Mallesh M. S/O. Hanumanthappa
court: High Court of Karnataka, Dharwad Bench
judgment_date: DATED THIS THE 15TH DAY OF JANUARY, 2025

compensation_breakdown.interest: 6% p.a. from date of petition till realization

facts: On 28.07.2011 at about 5.45 p.m. at Buden Sab Huvinahadagali the claimant was sitting on the mud-guard of a tractor engine while the tractor was being used for tilling land. The driver (respondent No.1) drove rashly and lost control causing the tractor to turtled; the claimant sustained fractures to his left femur/thigh/leg and other injuries and received treatment at Government Hospital, Huvinahadagali. The claimant filed a claim petition before the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal seeking compensation.
arguments_claimant: The claimant argued the Tribunal under-assessed compensation and sought enhancement of the award; the claimant also sought that the Insurance Company be fastened with liability (pay and recover) instead of the owner alone. The claimant relied on injury evidence, disability and loss of income to justify a higher award.
arguments_respondent: The Insurance Company contended the claimant was sitting on the mud-guard of the tractor (as per the FIR) which is prohibited and not covered by the insurance policy, and therefore the insurer is not liable to indemnify; the owner alone must pay. The insurer relied on policy exclusions and traffic regulation breaches to contest liability.
ratio_decidendi: "A person who is sitting on the mud-guard of a Tractor is not required to be covered by the statutory insurance policy, as contemplated by Sub-section (1) of Section 147 of the M.V.Act." The Court held that when a person travels on the mud-guard as a gratuitous passenger that risk is not covered by the statutory insurance and therefore "the Insurance Company is not liable to indemnify the owner and the Insurance Company is liable to be exonerated for payment of compensation to the claimant." The Court further explained that the statutory remedy of "pay and recovery" under Section 149 is available only if the insurer's defence falls within the specific grounds in Sub-section (2) of Section 149, and mere violation of Regulation/Rule 28 (prohibition on carrying persons on a tractor mud-guard) does not bring the case within those Grounds; accordingly "order of pay and recovery cannot be made." The legal rule was applied to these facts because the claimant was proved to be sitting on the mud-guard and the tractor/trailer use did not fit the narrow circumstances required under s.149(2) to fasten insurer liability.
final_order: The appeal is allowed in part; the Tribunal award dated 16.06.2014 is modified and the claimant is awarded total compensation of Rs.2,33,680/- along with interest @ 6% p.a. from the date of petition till realization. Respondent No.2 (owner) is directed to deposit the compensation within eight weeks; the Insurance Company is exonerated from indemnifying the owner.
summary: This is a motor-accident compensation appeal where the claimant, injured after a tractor turtled on 28.07.2011 while he was sitting on the tractor mud-guard, sought enhancement and insurer liability. The High Court increased compensation from Rs.84,160/- to Rs.2,33,680/- (plus 6% interest) but held the insurer not liable because a person sitting on the mud-guard is not covered by statutory insurance under Section 147(1) and pay-and-recover under Section 149(2) was not attracted. Practically, the owner must pay the enhanced award and the insurer is exonerated.
embedding_summary: Dadapeera S/O Abdul Salam Sab v. Mallesh M (High Court of Karnataka, Dharwad Bench, MFA No.102485 of 2014; NC:2025:KHC-D:590) decided 15 January 2025. Accident on 28.07.2011 at Buden Sab Huvinahadagali: claimant sitting on tractor mud-guard while tilling; tractor bearing Reg.No.KA-35/T-9766 turtled; claimant sustained fractures and other injuries. Key issues: whether a person on a tractor mud-guard is covered under statutory insurance (Section 147 MV Act), whether insurer can be saddled with liability or entitled to defend under Section 149(2) MV Act, and applicability of Rule/Regulation 28 (driving of tractors prohibition). Court applied Full Bench authority (Gadhilingappa) and Apex Court decisions (V. Chinnamma, Pappu v. Vinod Kumar Lamba, National Insurance v. Swaran Singh) and held mud-guard passengers are not covered by s.147(1); pay-and-recover is available only when defences fall under s.149(2). Outcome: Tribunal award enhanced to Rs.2,33,680/- with 6% p.a. interest from petition date; owner to pay within eight weeks; insurer exonerated.
legal_principles: Person sitting on the mud-guard of a tractor is not covered by statutory insurance under Section 147(1) MV Act
Pay-and-recover under Section 149 is available only if insurer's defence falls within the specific grounds in Section 149(2)
Violation of Rule/Regulation 28 (no person to be carried on a tractor) does not automatically permit pay-and-recover under s.149(2)
A tractor attached to a trailer can constitute a transport vehicle for permit purposes (Sections 2(44) and 2(46)), but permit scope matters for insurer defence under s.149(2)
Where insurer is exonerated under the statutory scheme, the owner remains directly liable to pay compensation
sections_cited: Sub-section (1) of Section 147 of the Motor Vehicles Act
Sub-section (2) of Section 149 of the Motor Vehicles Act
Section 2(31) of the Motor Vehicles Act
Sections 2(44) and 2(46) of the Motor Vehicles Act
Rule 28 of Rules of Road Regulations, 1989 (Driving of tractors and goods vehicles)
Rule 2(b) and Rule 2(c) of the Central Motor Vehicle Rules, 1989
cases_cited: Gadhilingappa @ Gadhilinga and another vs. K. Guleppa and others, ILR 2021 KAR 3377 (Full Bench of Karnataka High Court)
National Insurance Co. Ltd. Vs. Sri Maruthi and Others (Division Bench of this Court)
PAPPU AND OTHERS Vs. VINOD KUMAR LAMBA AND ANOTHER, (2018) 3 SCC 308
NATIONAL INSURANCE COMPANY LIMITED VS. SWARAN SINGH AND OTHERS (2020) ACJ 2560
NEW INDIA ASSURANCE COMPANY LIMITED VS. YELLAVVA AND ANOTHER (2020 ACJ 2560)
bm25_text: Dadapeera S/O. Abdul Salam Sab vs Mallesh M. S/O. Hanumanthappa High Court of Karnataka, Dharwad Bench 2025 On 28.07.2011 at about 5.45 p.m. at Buden Sab Huvinahadagali the claimant was sitting on the mud-guard of a tractor engine while the tractor was being used for tilling land. The driver (respondent No.1) drove rashly and lost control causing the tractor to turtled; the claimant sustained fractures to his left femur/thigh/leg and other injuries and received treatment at Government Hospital, Huvinahadagali. The claimant filed a claim petition before the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal seeking compensation. The claimant argued the Tribunal under-assessed compensation and sought enhancement of the award; the claimant also sought that the Insurance Company be fastened with liability (pay and recover) instead of the owner alone. The claimant relied on injury evidence, disability and loss of income to justify a higher award. The Insurance Company contended the claimant was sitting on the mud-guard of the tractor (as per the FIR) which is prohibited and not covered by the insurance policy, and therefore the insurer is not liable to indemnify; the owner alone must pay. The insurer relied on policy exclusions and traffic regulation breaches to contest liability. "A person who is sitting on the mud-guard of a Tractor is not required to be covered by the statutory insurance policy, as contemplated by Sub-section (1) of Section 147 of the M.V.Act." The Court held that when a person travels on the mud-guard as a gratuitous passenger that risk is not covered by the statutory insurance and therefore "the Insurance Company is not liable to indemnify the owner and the Insurance Company is liable to be exonerated for payment of compensation to the claimant." The Court further explained that the statutory remedy of "pay and recovery" under Section 149 is available only if the insurer's defence falls within the specific grounds in Sub-section (2) of Section 149, and mere violation of Regulation/Rule 28 (prohibition on carrying persons on a tractor mud-guard) does not bring the case within those Grounds; accordingly "order of pay and recovery cannot be made." The legal rule was applied to these facts because the claimant was proved to be sitting on the mud-guard and the tractor/trailer use did not fit the narrow circumstances required under s.149(2) to fasten insurer liability. The appeal is allowed in part; the Tribunal award dated 16.06.2014 is modified and the claimant is awarded total compensation of Rs.2,33,680/- along with interest @ 6% p.a. from the date of petition till realization. Respondent No.2 (owner) is directed to deposit the compensation within eight weeks; the Insurance Company is exonerated from indemnifying the owner. This is a motor-accident compensation appeal where the claimant, injured after a tractor turtled on 28.07.2011 while he was sitting on the tractor mud-guard, sought enhancement and insurer liability. The High Court increased compensation from Rs.84,160/- to Rs.2,33,680/- (plus 6% interest) but held the insurer not liable because a person sitting on the mud-guard is not covered by statutory insurance under Section 147(1) and pay-and-recover under Section 149(2) was not attracted. Practically, the owner must pay the enhanced award and the insurer is exonerated. Dadapeera S/O Abdul Salam Sab v. Mallesh M (High Court of Karnataka, Dharwad Bench, MFA No.102485 of 2014; NC:2025:KHC-D:590) decided 15 January 2025. Accident on 28.07.2011 at Buden Sab Huvinahadagali: claimant sitting on tractor mud-guard while tilling; tractor bearing Reg.No.KA-35/T-9766 turtled; claimant sustained fractures and other injuries. Key issues: whether a person on a tractor mud-guard is covered under statutory insurance (Section 147 MV Act), whether insurer can be saddled with liability or entitled to defend under Section 149(2) MV Act, and applicability of Rule/Regulation 28 (driving of tractors prohibition). Court applied Full Bench authority (Gadhilingappa) and Apex Court decisions (V. Chinnamma, Pappu v. Vinod Kumar Lamba, National Insurance v. Swaran Singh) and held mud-guard passengers are not covered by s.147(1); pay-and-recover is available only when defences fall under s.149(2). Outcome: Tribunal award enhanced to Rs.2,33,680/- with 6% p.a. interest from petition date; owner to pay within eight weeks; insurer exonerated. Dadapeera S/O.
